texto: I’ll tell you what I don’t believe, Alwyn. I don’t believe there’s a pretty forest in the sky with castles and a white light and God and all his angels waiting to welcome all the good people in. And you and me standing there sick with nerves while they check us out to see if we’ve made the grade. That sounds too much like immigrating to Australia.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Beverly Rycroft: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a reflexao. O trecho “I’ll tell you what I don’t believe, Alwyn. I don’t believe there’s a pretty forest in the sky wit…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, death, heaven pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de luto e heaven.

frase_original: I’ll tell you what I don’t believe, Alwyn. I don’t believe there’s a pretty forest in the sky with castles and a white light and God and all his angels waiting to welcome all the good people in. And you and me standing there sick with nerves while they check us out to see if we’ve made the grade. That sounds too much like immigrating to Australia.

texto: This is what I find so strange: we are not necessarily kind to animals. We use them, we eat them. But we don’t like them to suffer. Yet humans must. They have to wait for the great Vet himself to decide how long their anguish must last and how deep it must reach. And He has, as far as I can see, a habit of waiting a long, long time before deciding to end their misery.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Beverly Rycroft: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de passagem do tempo e prioridades. O trecho “This is what I find so strange: we are not necessarily kind to animals. We use them, we eat them.…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que desenvolvimento-pessoal, animals, death pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de luto e termino.

And He has, as far as I can see, a habit of waiting a long, long time before deciding to end their misery.

texto: Shame lurks in the pockets of the mind (or spirit), like lint. No matter how brave a face you put on things – and believe me, I did – you always have to face yourself in the end.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Beverly Rycroft: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a fe. O trecho “Shame lurks in the pockets of the mind (or spirit), like lint.
